questions.question: Define viewing transformation in computer graphics and outline the sequence of operations that convert world coordinates to screen coordinates.
keyPoints: World to camera (view) transformation using view matrix.
Projection transformation (orthographic or perspective) to normalized device coordinates (NDC).
Clipping against the view volume in NDC space.
Viewport transformation maps NDC to screen (window) coordinates.

questions.question: Differentiate between orthographic (parallel) and perspective projections. Provide their basic projection equations and typical applications.
keyPoints: Orthographic: parallel lines remain parallel; equations x' = x, y' = y (no depth division).
Perspective: lines converge to vanishing points; equations x' = x/z, y' = y/z (division by depth).
Orthographic matrix is simple scaling/translation; used in CAD and technical drawings.
Perspective matrix includes focal length/fov; used for realistic rendering and visual simulation.

questions.question: Explain the camera model used in 3D computer graphics, describing how the view coordinate system is defined, how the view transformation matrix is constructed, and how orthographic and perspective projection matrices map the view volume to the canonical normalized device coordinate (NDC) cube.
keyPoints: Camera defined by eye position, look‑at point, up vector; view matrix built from basis vectors.
Orthographic projection matrix scales and translates the view volume to [-1,1]^3 (canonical cube).
Perspective projection matrix maps the frustum to the canonical perspective volume; homogeneous division yields NDC.
Combined view‑projection matrix transforms world coordinates directly to NDC, ready for clipping.

questions.question: Discuss the clipping process in the viewing pipeline. Describe the purpose of clipping and explain the Cohen‑Sutherland line clipping algorithm, including the use of outcodes, trivial accept/reject tests, and parametric intersection calculation. Also derive the viewport transformation that maps normalized device coordinates to a screen window defined by (left, bottom, width, height).
keyPoints: Clipping removes primitives outside the view volume to avoid rendering invisible parts.
Cohen‑Sutherland uses 4‑bit outcodes; trivial accept when both 0, trivial reject when bitwise AND non‑zero.
When not trivial, compute intersection parameter t using edge equations to clip the line.
Viewport mapping: x_s = (x_n+1)*(w/2)+x_min, y_s = (y_n+1)*(h/2)+y_min, accounting for screen origin (top‑left).
